fact: December 6 is exactly 25 days before New Year's Eve - that's like counting down Christmas presents but for the whole year ending!
In the Arctic, polar bears are typically getting chunky right now because they're eating seals on the newly frozen ice that usually forms in early December.
In most northern places, December 6 is often when morning temperatures first stay below freezing all day long - that's why your breath looks like a dragon's!
Pine trees' needles often turn extra dark green in early December to soak up every bit of weak winter sunlight.
People born on this date are Sagittarius, which means they're supposedly adventurous fire signs - pretty funny since it's getting cold and dark outside!
December 6 falls during the 'polar night' season in northern Alaska, where the sun doesn't rise at all and the sky stays purple-blue all day.
Squirrels go absolutely nuts around this time - they're frantically digging up their buried acorns because they can smell them under snow.
In Australia and South America, December 6 is when jacaranda trees typically explode with bright purple flowers because it's almost summer there.
The winter solstice is only 15 days away from December 6, which means every day the sun sets a little earlier and rises a little later in the Northern Hemisphere.
Owls are often having babies right now in warm climates because there are tons of mice running around looking for cozy winter hiding spots.
December 6 is usually in the middle of 'mud season' in places like the Pacific Northwest, where it rains almost every single day.
Your shadow at noon on December 6 is almost as long as you are tall if you live anywhere north of Mexico - the sun is super low in the sky.
